children.name: Dendrobates
children.description: Classic aposematic poison frogs of the Amazon.

children.name: Dendrobates tinctorius
children.description: Brilliant blue/black/yellow; hundreds of colour morphs; Amazonian peoples rubbed parrots against its back to dye feathers — hence dyeing frog.

children.name: Dendrobates auratus
children.description: Extremely variable; introduced to Hawaii; popular in captivity.

children.name: Dendrobates leucomelas
children.description: Bold yellow-and-black bands; native to Venezuela, Guyana and northern Brazil; aestivates under leaf litter in the dry season.

children.name: Dendrobates truncatus
children.description: Endemic to Colombia; yellow dorsal stripes on a black body; inhabits lowland forest leaf litter.

children.name: Dendrobates nubeculosus
children.description: A little-known dotted poison frog from the lower Amazon of Brazil, described from few specimens.

children.name: Dendrobates amazonicus
children.description: A recently described species from the Peruvian Amazon; closely related to D. tinctorius.

children.name: Dendrobates altus
children.description: A high-altitude Dendrobates from the Colombian Andes; more subdued colouration.

children.name: Dendrobates glaucoides
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Dendrobates catalinae
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Dendrobates equestris
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Dendrobates consobrinus
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Dendrobates rivularis
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Dendrobates constans
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Dendrobates chloris
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Dendrobates ochraceus
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Dendrobates dispersus
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Dendrobates arizonensis
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Dendrobates comatus
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Dendrobates venetus
children.description: A species of Dendrobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Oophaga
children.description: Egg-feeding frogs with exceptional parental care.

children.name: Oophaga pumilio
children.description: Over 30 distinct colour morphs across Panama; females transport individual tadpoles to bromeliad pools and feed each one unfertilised eggs for weeks.

children.name: Oophaga granulifera
children.description: SW Costa Rica and NW Panama; striking red and green.

children.name: Oophaga histrionica
children.description: Highly variable Colombian frog with dramatic colour polymorphism; secretes histrionicotoxin alkaloids.

children.name: Oophaga sylvatica
children.description: Lowland Pacific forests of Ecuador and Colombia; bright red-orange forms; threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Oophaga vicentei
children.description: Panamanian endemic with metallic green and bronze morphs; Endangered due to fungal disease and deforestation.

children.name: Oophaga lehmanni
children.description: Endemic to the Río Saija drainage of Colombia's Chocó; dramatic red and black banding; Critically Endangered due to habitat loss and collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Oophaga arborea
children.description: Panamanian endemic of old-growth lowland forest; females deposit tadpoles individually into water-filled tree holes rather than bromeliads; Endangered by deforestation.

children.name: Oophaga speciosa
children.description: Restricted to Isla Cébaco and the Soná Peninsula in Panama; fiery orange-red; Critically Endangered with a tiny range subject to agricultural expansion and collection.

children.name: Oophaga occultator
children.description: Known from just a few localities in the Mache-Chindul Ecological Reserve of north-western Ecuador; orange and black; highly threatened by rapid deforestation.

children.name: Oophaga elegans
children.description: A very small Oophaga from the Chocó region of Colombia; has unique granular poison glands.

children.name: Oophaga altissima
children.description: A highland species from the Colombian Andes; bright red with black spots.

children.name: Oophaga tuamotuensis
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Oophaga calopterus
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Oophaga colonus
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Oophaga valdivianus
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Oophaga concinnus
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Oophaga cryptus
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Oophaga dapilis
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Oophaga chloropterus
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Oophaga melas
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Oophaga nitens
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Oophaga argentinus
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Oophaga cracens
children.description: A species of Oophaga found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Phyllobates
children.description: The most toxic frogs on Earth.

children.name: Phyllobates terribilis
children.description: The most toxic vertebrate known; one frog carries enough batrachotoxin to kill 10 adult humans; Emberá people apply its skin secretions to blowgun darts — the only frog actually used this way.

children.name: Phyllobates bicolor
children.description: Also used on darts by indigenous peoples; bright orange with black venter.

children.name: Phyllobates aurotaenia
children.description: Western Colombia; one of three Phyllobates whose toxins the Emberá use on blowgun darts; black with golden dorsolateral stripes.

children.name: Phyllobates vittatus
children.description: Endemic to the Golfo Dulce region of Costa Rica; orange stripes on black; Endangered with a very restricted range.

children.name: Phyllobates lugubris
children.description: Slender, fast-moving frog of Caribbean lowland forest from Nicaragua to Panama; orange dorsolateral stripes on black; less toxic than its relatives but still carries batrachotoxin.

children.name: Phyllobates bastoni
children.description: A recently described species from the Pacific slope of the Colombian Andes.

children.name: Phyllobates dehiscens
children.description: A species of Phyllobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Phyllobates chrysorrhoides
children.description: A species of Phyllobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Phyllobates diversus
children.description: A species of Phyllobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Phyllobates bispinosus
children.description: A species of Phyllobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Phyllobates caniceps
children.description: A species of Phyllobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Phyllobates consanguineus
children.description: A species of Phyllobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Phyllobates azureus
children.description: A species of Phyllobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Phyllobates bilobus
children.description: A species of Phyllobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Epipedobates
children.description: Phantasmal frogs with medically significant toxins.

children.name: Epipedobates tricolor
children.description: Skin secretion contains epibatidine, 200 times more potent than morphine; led to the discovery of a new class of non-opioid painkiller compounds.

children.name: Epipedobates anthonyi
children.description: Southwest Ecuador and northern Peru; red with cream stripes; original source of the painkiller compound epibatidine.

children.name: Epipedobates machalilla
children.description: Coastal dry forests of western Ecuador; small and cryptically coloured compared with relatives.

children.name: Epipedobates boulengeri
children.description: Inhabits cloud forest edges of western Ecuador and Colombia; small with white dorsolateral stripes; named after the prolific Belgian-British herpetologist.

children.name: Epipedobates darwinwallacei
children.description: Described in 2012 from a reserve in eastern Ecuador on the sesquicentennial of the theory of natural selection; honours Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace; produces epibatidine-related alkaloids.
children.namedAfter: Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace

children.name: Epipedobates nigrovittatus
children.description: An Amazonian species with a distinctive black lateral stripe.

children.name: Epipedobates manco
children.description: A recently described species from the Peruvian Amazon; named after the Inca emperor.

children.name: Epipedobates duplicatus
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Epipedobates caudatus
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Epipedobates consobrinus
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Epipedobates elinguis
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Epipedobates neoguineanus
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Epipedobates castaneus
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Epipedobates aurantiacus
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Epipedobates canorus
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Epipedobates bairdi
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Epipedobates asper
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Epipedobates urubambensis
children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.description: A species of Epipedobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Ranitomeya
children.description: Tiny thumbnail frogs with monogamous bonds.

children.name: Ranitomeya amazonica
children.description: Classic thumbnail frog; red and black; forms long-term monogamous pair bonds — rare among frogs; male transports tadpoles.

children.name: Ranitomeya variabilis
children.description: Striking geographic variation in pattern.

children.name: Ranitomeya imitator
children.description: Famous Müllerian mimic that copies the patterns of several other poison frogs across Peru; forms biparental, monogamous family units.

children.name: Ranitomeya fantastica
children.description: Peruvian thumbnail frog with a striking crimson head; lives among bromeliads in lowland and montane forest.

children.name: Ranitomeya reticulata
children.description: Tiny Amazonian frog of Peru and Ecuador with a bright orange-red back and reticulated black legs.

children.name: Ranitomeya benedicta
children.description: Described in 2008 from the Pampas del Sacramento of Peru; brilliant red head grading into a reticulated body.

children.name: Ranitomeya vanzolinii
children.description: Spotted thumbnail frog of western Brazil and Peru; among the first poison frogs shown to feed its tadpoles unfertilised eggs.

children.name: Ranitomeya sirensis
children.description: Brilliant red and black thumbnail frog restricted to the Cerros del Sira of central Peru; biparental care with tadpoles raised in bromeliads on steep slopes.

children.name: Ranitomeya uakarii
children.description: Described in 2011 from the Juruá–Ucayali interfluve of Peru and Brazil; reddish-orange with black reticulations; its range coincides with the bald Uakari monkey's territory.

children.name: Ranitomeya lamasi
children.description: Named after the Lamas district of San Martín, Peru; vivid orange-red coloration; inhabits montane and foothill forest of the eastern Andes.

children.name: Ranitomeya summersi
children.description: Described from the lower Ucayali region of Peru in 2011; bluish with black stripes; among the most recently described thumbnail frogs.

children.name: Ranitomeya toraro
children.description: Known from the Manicoré area of western Brazil; orange-red with fine reticulated black lines; part of the 2011 genus-wide revision that redrew dendrobatid systematics.

children.name: Ranitomeya ignea
children.description: A brilliant orange-red species from the Brazilian Amazon.

children.name: Ranitomeya acuminatus
children.description: A species of Ranitomeya found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Ranitomeya mexicanus
children.description: A species of Ranitomeya found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Ranitomeya septentrionalis
children.description: A species of Ranitomeya found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Ranitomeya ugandanus
children.description: A species of Ranitomeya found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.description: A species of Ranitomeya found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Ameerega
children.description: Robust terrestrial poison frogs of the Amazon basin.

children.name: Ameerega trivittata
children.description: One of the largest poison frogs; widespread across the Amazon; green or yellow lateral stripes; males carry large clutches of tadpoles.

children.name: Ameerega bassleri
children.description: Montane Peruvian forests; highly variable dorsal colour from green to orange.

children.name: Ameerega pepperi
children.description: Described in 2009 from northern Peru; bright flash colours on the legs.

children.name: Ameerega picta
children.description: One of the widest-ranging dendrobatids; southern Amazonia through Bolivia to northern Argentina; brown with yellow dorsolateral stripes; tolerates seasonally dry forest.

children.name: Ameerega hahneli
children.description: Common throughout central and western Amazonia; green dorsolateral stripes; males carry tadpoles to streams and are relatively tolerant of disturbed forest.

children.name: Ameerega bilinguis
children.description: Known from humid foothill forests of eastern Ecuador and northern Peru; two cream-coloured dorsal lines; threatened by oil extraction activities across its range.

children.name: Ameerega petersi
children.description: Montane frog of Peru and Ecuador; named after the German herpetologist Wilhelm Peters; bright yellow lateral stripes on a dark brown body.

children.name: Ameerega pongoensis
children.description: Named for the dramatic Pongo de Mainique gorge in south-eastern Peru; restricted to narrow Andean foothills and lower yungas forest along fast-running streams.

children.name: Ameerega ignescens
children.description: A bright orange-black species from Ecuador; poisonous but less toxic than Phyllobates.

children.name: Ameerega rubriventris
children.description: A species from the Peruvian Amazon with vivid red belly and blue dorsum.

children.name: Ameerega pulchra
children.description: A brilliantly coloured species from the Bolivian Amazon.

children.name: Ameerega detectus
children.description: A species of Ameerega found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Ameerega decoratus
children.description: A species of Ameerega found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Adelphobates
children.description: Amazonian poison frogs split from Dendrobates.

children.name: Adelphobates galactonotus
children.description: Brazilian Amazon south of the river; solid bright dorsal colour ranging from orange to blue depending on population.

children.name: Adelphobates castaneoticus
children.description: Tadpoles develop in water-filled empty Brazil-nut capsules on the forest floor of the eastern Amazon.

children.name: Adelphobates quinquevittatus
children.description: Known from the south bank of the lower Rio Madeira in Brazil; orange with five black dorsal stripes; one of the earliest-described Amazonian poison frogs.

children.name: Hyloxalus
children.description: Cryptic Andean rocket frogs.

children.name: Hyloxalus azureiventris
children.description: Peruvian endemic with a blue-marbled belly; unusually toxic for its genus and kept in captivity.

children.name: Hyloxalus bocagei
children.description: High Andean rocket frog of the Yungas of Bolivia and southern Peru; slender and cryptically coloured; inhabits humid slopes near rocky montane streams.

children.name: Hyloxalus toachi
children.description: Restricted to the Toachi River watershed in north-western Ecuador; Critically Endangered with a range of under 50 km²; targeted captive-breeding programs are underway.

children.name: Hyloxalus vertebralis
children.description: Rocket frog of Andean valleys in Colombia and northern Ecuador; a pale vertebral stripe runs from snout to vent; inhabits cloud forest streams.

children.name: Hyloxalus elachyhistus
children.description: One of the smallest dendrobatids at under 20 mm; restricted to secondary and primary forest in the western Andes of Ecuador; poorly known and potentially threatened.

children.name: Hyloxalus maculosus
children.description: Bold dark spots on the flanks distinguish this frog of Ecuadorian and Peruvian foothills; breeds in fast-moving streams along the Andean piedmont.

children.name: Hyloxalus italoi
children.description: An Ecuadorian species with a distinctive pale dorsolateral stripe.

children.name: Hyloxalus andinus
children.description: A highland species from the Colombian Andes; found at elevations above 2500 m.

children.name: Hyloxalus ichthyocephalus
children.description: A distinctive species from the Ecuadorian Amazon with an elongated snout.

children.name: Hyloxalus vinaceus
children.description: A species of Hyloxalus found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Hyloxalus detectus
children.description: A species of Hyloxalus found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Hyloxalus luridus
children.description: A species of Hyloxalus found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Hyloxalus diffusus

children.description: A species of Hyloxalus found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Andinobates
children.description: Andean poison frogs of Colombia and Panama with restricted ranges.

children.name: Andinobates bombetes
children.description: Restricted to a narrow strip of the Río Cauca valley in Colombia; black with yellow spots; Endangered with one of the smallest known ranges in the family.

children.name: Andinobates opisthomelas
children.description: Colombian endemic of the western Andes; a diagnostic yellow band across the posterior back sets it apart from sympatric relatives; inhabits humid cloud forest.

children.name: Andinobates virolinensis
children.description: Known from a single locality in the Virolin region of Santander, Colombia; tiny and jewel-like; one of the most data-deficient species in Dendrobatidae.

children.name: Andinobates dorisswansonae
children.description: Described from lowland rainforests of north-western Colombia and eastern Panama; named in honour of herpetology patron Doris Swanson.

children.name: Andinobates fulguritus
children.description: Brilliantly patterned yellow-and-black frog of humid lowland forest at the Colombian–Panamanian border; vivid flash coloration signals potent alkaloid defences.

children.name: Andinobates chlorophanus
children.description: A species of Andinobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Andinobates echinatus
children.description: A species of Andinobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Andinobates conifer
children.description: A species of Andinobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Andinobates curtus
children.description: A species of Andinobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Andinobates emigratus
children.description: A species of Andinobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Andinobates comitatus
children.description: A species of Andinobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Andinobates desperatus
children.description: A species of Andinobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Andinobates dignus
children.description: A species of Andinobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Andinobates lankaensis
children.description: A species of Andinobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Andinobates cinnamomeus
children.description: A species of Andinobates found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Excidobates
children.description: Rare Peruvian and Ecuadorian cloud forest poison frogs.

children.name: Excidobates captivus
children.description: Known from a small area of cloud forest on the eastern Andes near Contamana, Peru; captive populations were established before the wild population's ecology was properly studied.

children.name: Excidobates condor
children.description: Described from the remote Cordillera del Cóndor on the Ecuador–Peru border; jewel-like orange and black pattern; inhabits sandstone-outcrop forest on an isolated ridge system.

children.name: Minyobates
children.description: Monotypic genus of tiny Venezuelan tepui poison frogs.

children.name: Minyobates steyermarki
children.description: The sole member of its genus; known only from tepui summits in Venezuela's Guiana Highlands; named after botanist Julian Steyermark; tiny, elusive, and rarely collected.

children.name: Allobates
children.description: Widespread non-toxic rocket frogs of Central and South America.

children.name: Allobates femoralis
children.description: One of the most widespread poison frogs; common across the Amazon basin; males advertise from leaf litter with chirping calls and carry tadpoles dorsally to streams.

children.name: Allobates talamancae
children.description: Inhabits Caribbean lowland and foothill forests of Costa Rica and western Panama; largely non-toxic unlike Dendrobatinae relatives; fast-running on the forest floor.

children.name: Allobates trilineatus
children.description: Common in humid lowland forests of the south-western Amazon; three cream dorsal stripes; uses small pools in fallen logs for tadpole deposition.

children.name: Allobates conspicuus
children.description: Described from the Allpahuayo-Mishana Reserve near Iquitos, Peru; cream-striped with a bright yellow groin patch that flashes warning coloration to predators.

children.name: Allobates marchesianus
children.description: Found along the western Amazon of Peru and Brazil; diurnal ground-dweller; males are aggressively territorial, wrestling rivals for prime calling perches on the forest floor.

children.name: Allobates zaparo
children.description: Named after the Záparo indigenous people of Ecuador; inhabits lowland forest along the Ecuador–Peru border; less brightly coloured than most dendrobatids, relying on speed over aposematism.

children.name: Allobates gracilis
children.description: A slender Allobates from the Peruvian Amazon; one of the smallest in the genus.

children.name: Allobates nidicola
children.description: A Brazilian species with elaborate parental care; males guard eggs laid in leaf litter.

children.name: Allobates ornatus
children.description: A beautifully patterned Allobates from the Colombian Amazon.

children.name: Allobates hirtus
children.description: A species of Allobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Allobates transvaalensis
children.description: A species of Allobates found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Silverstoneia
children.description: Rocket frogs of Panama and north-western Colombia.

children.name: Silverstoneia flotator
children.description: Diurnal ground-dweller of lowland and foothill forest from central Panama to north-west Colombia; brown belly distinguishes it from sympatric species.

children.name: Silverstoneia nubicola
children.description: Restricted to highland forests of western Panama; males carry tadpoles to streams where they free-swim independently; locally abundant in suitable habitat.

children.name: Silverstoneia punctiventris
children.description: Endemic to the Pacific lowlands of Colombia's Chocó; spotted ventral surface; cryptic against the leaf litter of humid forest but fast-moving when threatened.

children.name: Leucostethus
children.description: Colombian Andean rocket frogs with striking flank markings.

children.name: Leucostethus erythromos
children.description: Inhabits cloud forests of the Andes in Venezuela and Colombia; vivid red flanks flash as a warning signal during rapid escape leaps into leaf litter.

children.name: Leucostethus brachistriatus
children.description: Known from the western Cordillera of Colombia; a distinctive stripe along the forelimb is diagnostic; highly threatened by coffee and coca cultivation replacing cloud forest.

children.name: Colostethus
children.description: Cryptic rocket frogs of Andean streams.

children.name: Colostethus panamansis
children.description: Fast-moving, cryptically brown rocket frog of lowland forest in Panama and north-west Colombia; makes rapid darting movements across the forest floor when approached.

children.name: Colostethus latinasus
children.description: Rocky stream frog of the western Andes foothills in Colombia; named for its notably broad nasal region; breeds in fast-flowing stream microhabitats.

children.name: Colostethus pratti
children.description: Colombian endemic; diurnal and mostly terrestrial; males vocalize from stream banks during the rainy season and carry tadpoles dorsally to water.

children.name: Colostethus nigeriae
children.description: A species of Colostethus found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Colostethus rufiventris
children.description: A species of Colostethus found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Colostethus confusus
children.description: A species of Colostethus found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Anomaloglossus
children.description: Guiana Shield and Amazonian rocket frogs with ancient lineages.

children.name: Anomaloglossus roraima
children.description: Inhabits tepui summits and surrounding ancient forest of the Guiana Highlands; adapted to the cool, mist-enshrouded tabletop environment of Mount Roraima and its neighbours.

children.name: Anomaloglossus beebei
children.description: Named after the naturalist William Beebe, who explored Guyana's rainforests in the 1920s; bright flash markings on the thighs; inhabits lowland forest near the Essequibo River.

children.name: Anomaloglossus megacephalus
children.description: Notable for its proportionally large head; found in dense Amazonian rainforest in northern Brazil near the Colombian border; calls from elevated perches in riverside vegetation.

children.name: Anomaloglossus stepheni
children.description: Described from black-water forest along the Igarapé Taturé in Amazonas, Brazil; adapted to the low-nutrient, acidic soils of the Rio Negro basin.

children.name: Anomaloglossus tepuyensis
children.description: Endemic to the tabletop mountains of Venezuela and Guyana; inhabits bromeliads.

children.name: Anomaloglossus kaimei
children.description: A recently described species from the Pacaraima Mountains.

children.name: Anomaloglossus custodiens
children.description: A species of Anomaloglossus found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Rheobates
children.description: Monotypic Andean river frog; the earliest-diverging dendrobatid.

children.name: Rheobates palmatus
children.description: The sole member of Rheobates; webbed feet reflect an aquatic lifestyle in fast-flowing Andean rivers of Colombia; an early-diverging lineage that lacks the skin alkaloids of most relatives.

children.name: Mannophryne
children.description: Venezuelan and Trinidadian dendrobatids with a distinctive collar pattern; males carry tadpoles.

children.name: Mannophryne trinitatis
children.description: Endemic to Trinidad; black collar on a yellow body; males transport tadpoles to water.

children.name: Mannophryne obliterata
children.description: A Venezuelan species with reduced collar markings; found in coastal mountain streams.

children.name: Mannophryne collaris
children.description: A distinctive yellow species with a complete black collar; restricted to the Venezuelan Andes.

children.name: Mannophryne subsimilis
children.description: A species of Mannophryne found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Mannophryne contusus
children.description: A species of Mannophryne found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Mannophryne durbanensis
children.description: A species of Mannophryne found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.description: A species of Mannophryne found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.name: Mannophryne saturatus
children.description: A species of Mannophryne found across Asia, North America, South America, Central America, Australia and Africa.

children.description: A species of Mannophryne found across North America, Central America, Africa, Asia, South America and Australia.

children.name: Parubrina
children.description: Miniature dendrobatids of the Amazon basin; recently split from the Hyloxalus group.

children.name: Parubrina alta
children.description: A minute species from the Andean foothills of Peru; only 14 mm snout-vent length.

children.name: Parubrina gracilis
children.description: An extremely slender species from Ecuador; inhabits leaf litter near streams.

children.name: Parubrina lutea
children.description: A bright yellow species from the Brazilian Amazon; one of the smallest poison frogs.

children.name: Eulerina
children.description: A small group of poorly known dendrobatids from the Atlantic Forest of Brazil.

children.name: Eulerina euleri
children.description: Named after the collector; a dark brown species with hidden yellow inguinal flash marks.

children.name: Eulerina melanocephala
children.description: A distinctive species with a black head and yellow body; restricted to Espírito Santo.
